related_questions.snippet: RAG pipelines typically involve four steps: document preparation and chunking, vector indexing, retrieval and prompt augmentation.
related_questions.title: What is Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)? - Databricks

text_blocks.snippet: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI framework that improves Large Language Model (LLM) responses by grounding them on external, authoritative knowledge bases before generating an answer. It fetches relevant facts to ensure accuracy, reduce "hallucinations," and access up-to-date information without requiring costly model retraining.
snippet_highlighted_words: an AI framework that improves Large Language Model (LLM) responses by grounding them on external, authoritative knowledge bases before generating an answer

text_blocks.snippet: Instead of relying solely on the fixed data a model was initially trained on, a RAG system follows a specific sequence:

list.snippet: Retrieval: When you ask a question, the system scans an external repository or database (like company documents, a vector database, or the web) for the most relevant information.

Augmentation:

The system combines your original query with the retrieved documents to provide rich, accurate context.
Generation: The language model synthesizes this combined information to generate a precise, factual, and context-aware final response.
Factual Grounding: It reduces the likelihood of an AI making up false information ("hallucinations") by forcing it to cite specific external sources.
Real-time Updates: Static AI models have cutoff dates for their training data, but RAG systems can access the most current, up-to-date data available.
Domain Specificity: It allows generic models to answer highly specialized questions (e.g., medical or legal documents) by pulling from custom databases.

text_blocks.snippet: RAG is widely used across many modern AI systems, including:

list.snippet: Enterprise Search: Allowing employees to search internal company wikis, databases, or PDFs using natural language.

Customer Support Chatbots:
Providing accurate answers drawn directly from a brand's support documentation or FAQ pages.
Research Assistants: Summarizing multiple external articles or research papers with verifiable citations.

text_blocks.snippet:
For a deeper dive into how this framework is implemented technically, you can review
IBM Research's RAG overview
or explore AWS's guide on RAG
.
Are you looking to build a RAG system, or would you like to explore popular tools and vector databases used to implement this technology?

perspectives.author: bashi_fuirkashi
perspectives.title: Comment “TOPICS” to get a full guide on what you actually need to learn. RAG is the first thing everyone learns. It’s also where most people stop. And I get why. It’s the topic all over your feed right now, so it feels like the whole job. But when 1,700+ real AI engineering job descriptions were reverse-engineered, RAG was one line item in a much longer list. If you already know how to build software, here’s the AI-specific layer that actually gets you hired: Agents and orchestration. Chaining tools, managing state, and building workflows that survive production. LangGraph, MCP, the full stack. Evaluation. If you can’t measure whether your system got better or worse, you’re guessing. This is what separates a demo from a real product. LLMOps. Deployment, monitoring, versioning, cost control. The unglamorous work that keeps you employed. Safety and guardrails. Companies won’t ship what they can’t trust, so knowing how to handle this is a hiring signal on its own. ML foundations.
